body_txt: If you can't or won't lie, it can be hard to keep your secrets. But fear not, because there are ways to pull the wool over another's eyes without speaking a single untruth. After all, some truths are inherently implausible ; if you're really a pink rabbit from Mars, you might as well just say so. And if you can say it sarcastically ("What? Don't you see the ears?"), all the better. But if you're clever enough with your phrasing, you can speak clearly and honestly and still make your listeners hear something else. Sure, Alice may eventually catch wise, but when she accuses you of deceiving her, just point out that your Exact Words were nothing but the truth. She should pay more attention next time (if there is a next time...) A character who is told "you'll get what's coming to you" or "you'll get your just deserts" is almost always on the receiving end of this trope. In fact, such lines feature almost exclusively in comedies , since it's just too implausible that characters in a serious drama (i.e. responding to dangerous situations with appropriate caution) wouldn't recognize the threatening subtext behind such phrasing. Needless to say, a villain who prides himself on his honesty or social savvy considers this good form ("Sorry, Alice can't come to the phone right now. She's all tied up at the moment "). But heroic characters can give False Reassurance too, particularly when they have a secret to keep, but can't or won't lie to their loved ones' faces . For example, Clark Kent might tell Lois Lane, "Don't worry, Clark Kent won't be seen outside this tropical resort" — after all, it'll be Superman who goes out to investigate the mysterious temple. The key trope to many a Deal with the Devil . It also is the cause of much concern for the Worrisome "Mostly-ish" . Subtrope to Double Meaning . Compare Prophecy Twist , No Man of Woman Born (where a prophecy of triumph or doom gives False Reassurance to its target because it seems like it can never come true), Metaphorically True , Exact Words , Bad News in a Good Way , Doctors Teasing Patients , Lying by Omission , Stealth Insult , and You Said You Would Let Them Go . See also New Era Speech and Worrying for the Wrong Reason . The Self-Poisoning Gambit often relies on this. Contrast Suspiciously Specific Denial , Assurance Backfire , and Empty Promise . Not to be confused with "Not Your Fault" Reassurance .
